homeContent.architectureBody: The work starts with intent and domain clarity, moves through product and architecture decisions, and reaches production through implementation, verification, and explicit release authority.

architectureStages.label: Business communication
architectureStages.text: Stakeholder conversations, goals, constraints, and domain models define what the system must make possible.

architectureStages.label: End-to-end product engineering
architectureStages.text: Product decisions connect experience, frontend depth, data, workflows, and operating needs into one coherent system.

architectureStages.label: Backend systems
architectureStages.text: APIs, auth, business rules, data ownership, and operational workflows make the product reliable beyond its interface.

architectureStages.label: AI-native delivery
architectureStages.text: Agents execute across the lifecycle; tests, review, browser QA, preview, and human release accountability keep the work trustworthy.

architectureStages.label: System architecture
architectureStages.text: Clear boundaries, observability, release paths, and operational surfaces let the product evolve without losing accountability.

homeContent.practiceHeading: Tell me what you are building.
homeContent.practiceBody: I can join a team, shape a new product, modernize a system already in motion, or help turn a difficult delivery path into working software.

practicePoints.label: Product systems
practicePoints.text: Shape the product idea, interface, workflows, data, and operating model into one coherent experience.

practicePoints.label: Architecture & modernization
practicePoints.text: Evolve an existing system with clearer boundaries, modern capabilities, and room for the product to grow.

practicePoints.label: Delivery acceleration
practicePoints.text: Combine advanced AI workflows, previews, quality gates, observability, and cloud operations into everyday delivery.

homeContent.contactBody: Tell me what you are building, who it should serve, and what is getting in the way. I will read the context myself and reply with useful questions and a practical next step.

case-studies.title: Selected work
case-studies.summary: Live sites, active product builds, enterprise systems, and the engineering decisions behind each result.
case-studies.seoDescription: Selected work by Alejandro Ortiz Corro across production websites, active product builds, business systems, enterprise delivery, and modern product engineering.

case-study:eteria.title: ETERIA event-business product system
case-study:eteria.summary: A client project in production connecting public discovery, lead capture, proposals, private operations, and an internal SwiftUI client.
case-study:eteria.seoDescription: ETERIA is a client product in production with a public event landing, lead and WhatsApp conversion, public proposals, private web operations, Convex, PostHog, SwiftUI, and a protected Release Train.

caseStudyContent.overview: ETERIA is a client project in production for an event business. It connects a public landing and real lead paths with proposals and private operational workflows.

problem.title: Opportunity
problem.body: The business needed one coherent path from first public contact through a proposal and into the day-to-day work required to deliver an event.

businessOutcome.title: Business outcome
businessOutcome.body: The production system gives the team a public place to earn trust, capture leads through forms and WhatsApp, share versioned proposals, and coordinate work behind authenticated boundaries.

role.title: Role
role.body: Product direction and end-to-end implementation across the landing, lead capture, public proposals, private web application, operational workflows, shared backend, internal SwiftUI client, and release process.

constraints.title: Design considerations
constraints.body: The public story must prove a working product while keeping sensitive operational material inside its intended access boundaries. Only public landing art and sanitized system descriptions appear here.

architectureDecisions.title: Architecture decisions
architectureDecisions.body: Convex owns shared application state and workflows, PostHog supplies production observability, the public landing and proposals remain directly shareable, and the private web and SwiftUI surfaces stay behind their intended access boundaries.

executionHighlights.title: Execution highlights
executionHighlights.body: The product moved through a protected Release Train with development previews, production promotion, smoke checks, and explicit human authorization at release boundaries.

qualitySecurityPerformance.title: Quality, security, and performance
qualitySecurityPerformance.body: Public routes, lead conversion, analytics, backend state, privacy boundaries, and responsive behavior are verified as one system while private operational evidence remains sanitized or unpublished.

publicEvidence.label: Live ETERIA landing
publicEvidence.description: Open the public ETERIA experience in production.
publicEvidence.altText: ETERIA public event landing in production

confidentialityNote.title: Scope note
confidentialityNote.body: This case uses public and sanitized evidence only.

case-study:casa-roca.title: Casa Roca guest experience and conversion path
case-study:casa-roca.summary: A public hospitality experience in production with a clear story, direct contact path, and disciplined releases.
case-study:casa-roca.seoDescription: Casa Roca is a live hospitality website for a boutique stay in Veracruz, with clear place storytelling, direct guest inquiry paths, search-ready pages, and production release discipline.

caseStudyContent.overview: Casa Roca is the digital arrival point for a boutique stay in Roca Partida, Veracruz. It helps guests understand the place quickly, decide whether it fits their trip, and contact the business directly.

problem.title: Opportunity
problem.body: Casa Roca had the opportunity to turn its place, experience, and credibility into a clear digital arrival with a direct next step for guests.

businessOutcome.title: Business outcome
businessOutcome.body: The production site gives Casa Roca a clear destination for guests, search traffic, brand trust, and direct inquiries.

role.title: Role
role.body: Product direction, bilingual experience planning, SEO metadata, release verification, performance care, and the complete guest-facing delivery.

constraints.title: Design considerations
constraints.body: The experience brings speed, two languages, a tangible sense of place, and an unmistakable path to inquiry together for every guest.

architectureDecisions.title: Architecture decisions
architectureDecisions.body: Keep guest pages fast, treat bilingual content and search metadata as product concerns, and make direct contact the primary conversion path.

executionHighlights.title: Execution highlights
executionHighlights.body: The work covered hospitality positioning, localized routes, responsive art direction, production deployment, and verification on the real domain.

qualitySecurityPerformance.title: Quality, security, and performance
qualitySecurityPerformance.body: The live site uses explicit metadata, resilient layouts, optimized media, and stable pages that remain readable to guests and search engines.

publicEvidence.label: Live website
publicEvidence.description: Open the live Casa Roca website.

confidentialityNote.title: Scope note
confidentialityNote.body: This case focuses on the guest experience and production delivery.

case-study:the-barber-central.title: The Barber Central booking and operations system
case-study:the-barber-central.summary: A service-business SaaS approaching production across booking, customers, staff, inventory, reporting, analytics, and retention.
case-study:the-barber-central.seoDescription: The Barber Central is a service-business SaaS approaching production across booking, customers, staff, inventory, reporting, analytics, and retention workflows.

caseStudyContent.overview: The Barber Central is a SaaS product approaching production for barbershops, salons, and studios. Booking, customers, staff, inventory, reporting, analytics, and retention work as one operating system instead of a pile of tools.

problem.title: Opportunity
problem.body: Service businesses can bring bookings, reminders, staff operations, and payments into one generous daily workflow that gives growth more room.

businessOutcome.title: Business outcome
businessOutcome.body: The product direction centers the daily operating workflow: book clients, manage availability, communicate clearly, and keep the business moving without stitching tools together by hand.

role.title: Role
role.body: Product architecture, workflow modeling, full-stack implementation planning, booking flow decisions, payments readiness, customer communication, and quality gates for an active build.

constraints.title: Design considerations
constraints.body: Booking logic, staff availability, reminders, payments, and customer communication evolve together around the rhythm of the day.

architectureDecisions.title: Architecture decisions
architectureDecisions.body: Make the booking path simple, keep the operational dashboard task-first, and treat provider integrations as explicit business boundaries instead of hidden glue.

executionHighlights.title: Execution highlights
executionHighlights.body: The work covers booking IA, operations dashboard shape, provider setup, environment discipline, and manual browser QA for flows where auth, origin, and preview behavior matter.

qualitySecurityPerformance.title: Quality, security, and performance
qualitySecurityPerformance.body: The project emphasizes protected environments, explicit verification commands, browser QA, source checks, and careful handling of auth/provider configuration before public launch.

publicEvidence.label: Development preview
publicEvidence.description: Open the development landing page for The Barber Central.
publicEvidence.altText: The Barber Central development landing page

confidentialityNote.title: Scope note
confidentialityNote.body: This case follows the active product direction and release path.

case-study:nutri-plan.title: NutriPlan Digital clinical product system
case-study:nutri-plan.summary: A multi-surface clinical SaaS in testing and production preparation, with protected practitioner, administration, and mobile workflows.
case-study:nutri-plan.seoDescription: NutriPlan Digital is a multi-surface clinical SaaS in testing and production preparation across practitioner, administration, mobile, and shared backend workflows.

caseStudyContent.overview: NutriPlan Digital is a multi-surface clinical SaaS in testing and production preparation. It connects practitioner workflows, administration, mobile access, educational content, and operational clarity through shared backend state.

problem.title: Opportunity
problem.body: Nutrition teams can connect client records, plans, dashboards, and mobile experiences through one shared product model.

businessOutcome.title: Business outcome
businessOutcome.body: The build organizes the product around real operational roles and shared backend state so client work, admin work, and mobile access can come from one model.

role.title: Role
role.body: Product workflow architecture, dashboard planning, backend state modeling, mobile coordination, quality practice, and release discipline.

constraints.title: Design considerations
constraints.body: Health-related records call for thoughtful access boundaries, consistent workflow state, clear roles, and releases that respect ongoing client work.

architectureDecisions.title: Architecture decisions
architectureDecisions.body: Keep the backend as a shared source of truth, give dashboard and mobile workflows explicit responsibilities, and make environment and QA gates part of release readiness.

executionHighlights.title: Execution highlights
executionHighlights.body: The work spans landing, dashboard, admin dashboard, mobile app, Convex-backed state, route QA, preview verification, and operational parity with the reference system.

qualitySecurityPerformance.title: Quality, security, and performance
qualitySecurityPerformance.body: The project uses functional QA, route sweeps, environment separation, protected release branches, and preview validation when auth, routing, UI, or behavior changes.

publicEvidence.label: Sanitized preview
publicEvidence.description: Review the product screen and workflow notes.

confidentialityNote.title: Scope note
confidentialityNote.body: This case focuses on system responsibilities and delivery outcomes.

case-study:enterprise-systems.title: Enterprise product systems under operational pressure
case-study:enterprise-systems.summary: An internal customer-support and account-recovery operations platform and mission-critical emergency-response software show product judgment, performance work, and dependable delivery inside complex organizations.
case-study:enterprise-systems.seoDescription: Enterprise Product Systems covers an internal customer-support and account-recovery operations platform, a mission-critical emergency-response platform for a large US city, performance, workflows, observability, and dependable delivery without exposing private client details.

caseStudyContent.overview: An internal customer-support and account-recovery operations platform and mission-critical emergency-response software for a large US city show how product workflows, performance, observability, and delivery operate inside complex organizations.

problem.title: Opportunity
problem.body: Large systems create meaningful opportunities when ownership, integrations, operational workflows, and support paths are made visible.

businessOutcome.title: Business outcome
businessOutcome.body: The customer-support and account-recovery platform improved from approximately 3–5 seconds to under 1 second. Emergency-response workflows supported high-stakes dispatch where reliability and precise interaction mattered.

role.title: Role
role.body: In financial technology: stakeholder communication, roadmap and workflow definition, frontend depth, server-side and API work, observability, backend-oriented delivery, and Java work in progress. Earlier emergency-response work focused on custom keyboard dispatch and rigorous testing.

constraints.title: Operating considerations
constraints.body: Scale, integration design, data sensitivity, cross-team ownership, and production continuity shaped every decision.

architectureDecisions.title: Architecture decisions
architectureDecisions.body: Work from the business workflow backward, isolate risky integration points, keep operational contracts explicit, and favor maintainable backend paths over clever one-off fixes.

executionHighlights.title: Execution highlights
executionHighlights.body: Selected work includes payment-plan workflows, cashback implementation, customs handling, escalation ownership, and backend delivery across complex organizational contexts.

qualitySecurityPerformance.title: Quality, security, and performance
qualitySecurityPerformance.body: The work depends on careful change management, production awareness, data sensitivity, clear handoffs, and testing around workflows where mistakes become operational risk.

publicEvidence.label: Systems overview
publicEvidence.description: Review the enterprise delivery system through responsibilities, tradeoffs, and operational outcomes.

case-study:engineering-practice.title: AI-native development practice with human accountability
case-study:engineering-practice.summary: Agents work across requirements, domain, specs, tickets, implementation, review, QA, preview, and release while human judgment remains responsible.
case-study:engineering-practice.seoDescription: AI-Native Development Practice explains agents across requirements, domain, specs, tickets, implementation, review, QA, preview, and release with human accountability and private-tool boundaries.

caseStudyContent.overview: AI-native development means agents execute work across requirements, domain modeling, specs, tickets, implementation, review, QA, preview, and release while I remain accountable for decisions, quality, and release authority.

problem.title: Opportunity
problem.body: Modern software work moves quickly; strong architecture, tests, review, and production checks turn that energy into confident momentum.

businessOutcome.title: Business outcome
businessOutcome.body: A repeatable engineering process lets projects move faster while still producing inspectable decisions, safer releases, better handoffs, and stronger confidence for business stakeholders.

role.title: Role
role.body: I direct the work, review decisions and code, choose proportional gates, inspect browser behavior, and authorize release. Agents provide specialized execution throughout the lifecycle.

constraints.title: Design considerations
constraints.body: The Development System that coordinates this work is a private, adaptable tool—not open source, a commercial product, or a universal template. Public evidence comes from shipped systems and sanitized process artifacts.

architectureDecisions.title: Architecture decisions
architectureDecisions.body: Use clear seams, graph-backed public content, explicit environment contracts, protected branches, focused tests, and manual browser verification for visible behavior.

executionHighlights.title: Execution highlights
executionHighlights.body: The delivery system includes PR review, TDD tracers, visual checks, GitHub issue flow, release documentation, and browser QA.

qualitySecurityPerformance.title: Quality, security, and performance
qualitySecurityPerformance.body: Quality includes source checks, type checks, behavior tests, build verification, visual QA, and deployment paths that can be audited.

publicEvidence.label: Source and process
publicEvidence.description: Source, tests, documentation, and PR history show the engineering workflow behind the site.
publicEvidence.altText: Source and release process for AOHYS engineering practice

confidentialityNote.title: Scope note
confidentialityNote.body: This case focuses on the delivery system behind dependable releases.

practice.title: Three ways to move a product forward.
practice.summary: Build a complete product, add senior product-engineering capacity to a defined project, or modernize the system already carrying the business.
practice.seoDescription: Work with AOHYS to build a website, web app, or mobile app from zero; add a senior product engineer to a delivery team; or modernize an existing product with AI, data, cloud, and delivery capabilities.

practiceContent.heading: Bring the product ambition. Choose how we work.
practiceContent.deck: From a first sketch to a product ready for its next chapter, I join where senior product and engineering judgment can create momentum. Every engagement has a clear working rhythm, tangible outputs, and a system your team can keep improving.

services.title: Build a complete product from zero
services.problem: You have a real audience and a valuable idea, but the product path still needs to be shaped across experience, operations, data, architecture, and release.
services.result: A production-ready website, web application, or mobile application with a coherent customer journey, dependable workflows, measurable behavior, and room to grow.
services.engagement: I lead the product and engineering path from framing and system design through implementation, production launch, and an operating handoff your team can own.
deliverables: Working website, web app, or mobile app

services.title: Add a senior collaborator to your team
services.problem: The project and destination are defined, and the team wants experienced hands to accelerate product and architecture decisions and turn the plan into dependable software.
services.result: More delivery capacity without a long ramp-up: clearer decisions, stronger implementation, fewer blind spots, and a senior collaborator who improves the work around the code.
services.engagement: I embed with your existing product and engineering team for a focused phase, owning a meaningful workstream while collaborating in your planning, review, QA, and release rhythm.
deliverables: Tests, release evidence, and team handoff

services.title: Modernize what is already in motion
services.problem: An existing product or process already creates value, and the business is ready to expand it with clearer boundaries, connected data, modern workflows, and a stronger delivery path.
services.result: A safer, more capable system that preserves useful behavior while adding the right AI, data, cloud, observability, and delivery capabilities around it.
services.engagement: We map the current system, choose the highest-leverage boundary, and ship modernization in verified increments so the business keeps moving while the foundation improves.
deliverables: AI, data, cloud, or workflow integration
Incremental rollout, verification, and operating playbook

practiceContent.processHeading: Clear from the first working session.

process.label: Align
process.text: Confirm the outcome, audience, constraints, decision owners, and the evidence that will define progress.

process.label: Make the system visible
process.text: Map the experience, workflows, data, boundaries, risks, and tradeoffs before effort disappears into implementation.

process.label: Ship useful slices
process.text: Deliver complete, reviewable increments with real states, instrumentation, QA, and production checks.

process.label: Leave operating clarity
process.text: Hand over working software, explicit decisions, release evidence, and a practical next-step plan.

practiceContent.deliverablesHeading: Work you can keep using after the engagement.
deliverables: A shared product and system map
practiceContent.relatedHeading: Inspect the work and the system behind it.

architecture.title: Architecture
architecture.summary: The AI-native product-development lifecycle that carries business intent through domain, specs, agents, verification, preview, and production.
architecture.seoDescription: Alejandro Ortiz Corro's AI-native product-development architecture from business intent, grilling and domain modeling through specs, tickets, agents, tests, browser QA, preview, Release Train, and smoke checks.

architectureContent.heading: From business intent to production.
architectureContent.deck: AI-native product engineering is a disciplined lifecycle: agents execute meaningful work across every stage while I retain product judgment, accountability, and release authority.
architectureContent.systemBody: The sequence makes decisions, dependencies, verification, and release boundaries visible. AOHYS is one public implementation of this practice—not the limit of the architecture or a map of the private Development System.

layers.label: Business intent
layers.body: Listen to the business, clarify the user outcome, surface constraints, and grill ambiguous language before choosing a technical path.

layers.label: Domain & decisions
layers.body: Model the domain, name stable concepts, compare tradeoffs, and record the decisions that shape the product and system.

layers.label: Specs & dependency graph
layers.body: Turn the agreed direction into specs, executable tickets, ownership, acceptance seams, and dependencies that preserve the right order.

layers.label: Agent implementation
layers.body: Specialized agents research, implement, test, and review bounded work. The human orchestrator integrates evidence and resolves the decisions tools cannot own.

layers.label: Verification & browser QA
layers.body: Use proportional TDD, linting, type checks, behavior tests, independent review, and browser QA to verify the real user-visible result.

layers.label: Preview to production
layers.body: Build a Preview Deployment, collect human review, promote through the Release Train, verify the Production Deployment, and run smoke checks.

architectureContent.tradeoffsHeading: Architecture is a sequence of tradeoffs.

tradeoffs.title: Agent execution with human accountability
tradeoffs.reason: Agents can expand execution capacity, but they cannot own business intent, risk acceptance, or release authority.
tradeoffs.result: I remain responsible for what gets built, how it is verified, and whether it is ready to ship.

tradeoffs.title: Public explanation without publishing the private tool
tradeoffs.reason: The lifecycle should be inspectable without exposing private instructions, operating methods, or delivery details.
tradeoffs.result: The Development System remains private and adaptable; shipped products and sanitized artifacts provide the evidence.

tradeoffs.title: Preview before production
tradeoffs.reason: A green build does not prove that a release works in its real environment.
tradeoffs.result: Protected promotion, a Preview Deployment, human review, and smoke checks make production a separate decision.

sections.title: Human accountability
sections.body: Agents execute requirements work, modeling, specs, tickets, implementation, tests, review, and QA. Alejandro retains product judgment, integrates the evidence, and authorizes release.

sections.title: Public Source Site and Private Work
sections.body: The Public Source Site makes AOHYS implementation choices inspectable. Private Work—including client code, data, operational screens, credentials, and the Development System—remains private.

sections.title: Release Train
sections.body: A Preview Deployment is built and reviewed first. Production moves through a separate promotion with explicit authority, environment checks, deployment verification, and smoke checks on the canonical domain.

sections.title: AOHYS as a visible implementation
sections.body: AOHYS demonstrates localized public routes, authenticated private workflows, typed content, observability, and a protected release path. It is one concrete example of the broader professional architecture.

sections.title: Private Development System
sections.body: The Development System coordinates tools and agents across different coding environments. It is private, not open source or a commercial template, and every team should adapt its own practice.

sections.title: Production feedback
sections.body: Observability, analytics, errors, smoke checks, and stakeholder feedback return real behavior to the next product decision instead of treating release as the end of the lifecycle.

architectureContent.boundaryNote: This repository is a public working sample. It does not make private client code, product code, credentials, private screenshots, or operational data public.

resume.title: About Alejandro
resume.summary: Canonical experience for a Senior Software Engineer working across product systems, frontend depth, backend workflows, and AI-native delivery.
resume.seoDescription: Alejandro Ortiz Corro is a Senior Software Engineer focused on AI-native product development, enterprise systems, selected products, architecture, frontend depth, backend workflows, and accountable delivery.

resumeContent.name: Alejandro Ortiz Corro
resumeContent.role: Senior Software Engineer · AI-Native Product Development
resumeContent.intro: I turn business goals into product systems that ship. Frontend is a deep specialty; my current work also spans stakeholder communication, backend workflows, architecture, observability, and AI-native delivery with human accountability.

pdf.label: Open resume PDF

summary: Senior Software Engineer with 9+ years across enterprise systems, startup products, healthcare, commerce, service-business workflows, and public product experiences.

I combine business and stakeholder communication, end-to-end product engineering, backend workflows, architecture, and deep frontend experience in React, TypeScript, Next.js, performance, and accessible UI systems.
AI-native means agents execute across requirements, domain, specs, tickets, implementation, review, QA, and release while I retain human accountability for decisions, quality, and release authority.

highlights.label: Support and account-recovery platform performance
highlights.text: Helped reduce page loads from approximately 3–5 seconds to under 1 second through focused Next.js migration and performance work.

highlights.label: Mission-critical dispatch
highlights.text: Implemented custom keyboard dispatch workflows and comprehensive tests for emergency-response software serving a large US city.

highlights.label: AI-native delivery
highlights.text: Use agents across the lifecycle with TDD, independent review, browser QA, previews, Release Trains, smoke checks, and explicit human release authority.

highlights.label: Frontend depth, broader scope
highlights.text: Deep React, TypeScript, and interface experience now supports server-side/API work, observability, product workflows, and Java work in progress.

projects.title: Casa Roca
projects.summary: Public hospitality experience in production with place storytelling, search-ready pages, responsive media, and direct inquiry paths.
bullets: Delivered the guest-facing experience and verified it on the production domain.

Coordinate scope, roadmap, workflows, and success with stakeholders and multiple teams for an internal customer-support and account-recovery operations platform while retaining the contractual title Senior Frontend Developer.
Helped reduce platform loads from approximately 3–5 seconds to under 1 second; contributed to its modern Next.js/Turborepo evolution.
Deliver escalation, payment-plan, cashback, and other product workflows across frontend, server-side/API work, observability, and backend-oriented paths; expanding into Java.
Built and maintained a video-recording Chrome extension, including browser API and manifest constraints.
Delivered analytics dashboards and funnel visualizations for B2B marketing and sales workflows.
Delivered full-stack product features in a small medical-imaging startup team across marketing, sales, and result-presentation surfaces.
Consumed APIs and collaborated directly with design under healthcare privacy and security constraints; did not work on medical AI models.
Worked on the vehicle selector connecting automobile profiles to compatible parts within a large e-commerce platform.
Contributed to React SPA-to-Next.js modernization, React Native delivery, and marketing-defined SEO metadata.
Modernized a decades-old mission-critical emergency-response platform for a large US city using React and Redux.
Implemented custom keyboard dispatch workflows with rigorous tests and supported junior engineers in an international program.
Contributed to a billing surface within CEMEX GO and grew through a product distributed across many teams.
Built a continuous independent practice from Excel/VBA automation to Java, React and enterprise work, complete products, and AI-native development.
Current systems include ETERIA, The Barber Central, NutriPlan Digital, Casa Roca, and AOHYS.

contact.title: Tell me what you are building.
contact.summary: Share the product, role, or system you want to move forward. I will read the context myself and reply with useful questions and a practical next step. WhatsApp is fastest in Mexico; email is ideal for a longer brief.
contact.seoDescription: Contact Alejandro Ortiz Corro by email or WhatsApp to discuss a product, software-engineering role, architecture, modernization, or an AI-native delivery challenge.

privacy.title: Privacy
privacy.summary: How AOHYS treats contact data, analytics, errors, and private project information.
privacy.seoDescription: AOHYS privacy route explaining contact data, analytics, error reporting, private project boundaries, and public code limits.

privacyContent.heading: Privacy
privacyContent.intro: This site is public code as an engineering sample, but private client work and operational data stay private.

sections.title: Contact requests
sections.body: When you send the contact form, AOHYS uses your name, email, optional phone/company, preferred contact path, intent, and message only to respond to the request.

sections.title: Analytics and errors
sections.body: PostHog is used for explicit pageviews, selected CTA/form events, and browser error signals. Autocapture is disabled, and contact message text is not sent to analytics.

sections.title: Private project boundaries
sections.body: This repository is public for review. Private client code, credentials, operational records, and dashboard data are not public.

privacyContent.boundaryNote: The private dashboard under /dashboard is authenticated, noindexed, excluded from the sitemap, and separate from public SEO pages.
